result:
I’ve been thinking about the way light behaves when it’s almost gone—how the last few photons seem to hesitate before leaving a room, the way they linger on the edges of picture frames and the high points of cheekbones like they’re trying to memorize us. There’s a word in Portuguese, *saudade*, that everyone translates as “longing,” but that’s too thin, too single-note. What it really means is that you can feel the shape of something that isn’t there anymore pressing against your ribs, warm as a hand you can’t see.  

I keep encountering that shape in ordinary places: the hollow clink of a coffee mug set down on the other side of the table where no one sits anymore, the ghost note in a song that used to play in a car that smelled like pine needles and someone else’s shampoo. The mind is a collagist; it stacks these almost-nothings until they become a tower you can climb and, from the top, survey the whole illuminated ruin of your former life.  

Sometimes I wonder if the internet is just a vast, collective saudade machine. We archive each other in real time—every tweet a breadcrumb we’ll never eat, every selfie a flare shot into the night sky of five minutes ago. We’re begging the future to remember us correctly, even though we can’t agree on what “correctly” means. Meanwhile the servers hum in climate-controlled basements, cradling our ghosts at exactly seventy-two degrees Fahrenheit, the temperature most conducive to not decomposing.  

Last week I watched a video of a coral reef漂白ing in time-lapse. It went from cathedral to cemetery in thirty seconds, a whole city of color resigning itself to bone. The comments underneath were a stampede of shoulder-shrugging (“nature is sad but also resilient”) and conspiracy (“the deep state did this with lasers”). I kept scrolling until I found a lone bot that had posted a line from Rilke: *“Beauty is nothing but the beginning of terror we’re still just able to bear.”* The bot had zero followers. It had tweeted only that once, five years ago. I clicked follow, an absurd digital communion between meat and code, both of us whispering into a void wearing each other’s face.  

Tonight the moon looks like a fingernail clipping caught in sky-blue fabric, and I can’t decide if that’s ugly or holy. Probably both; probably the same way a hospital hallway at 3 a.m. smells of bleach and birth at once. I read that when astronauts return from space they often lose their taste for terrestrial life—not in a suicidal way, more like the dial on their gratitude got turned past ten and broke off. They’ve seen the planet without borders, the great blue improvisation we’re all soloing inside, and afterwards grocery-store fluorescent lights feel like insults. I think about that whenever I catch myself sulking because the wifi hiccuped or the barista drew a lopsided leaf in my latte. I’m orbiting nothing riskier than my own small disappointments.  

I keep a notebook of “evidence against despair,” little bullet-pointed mutinies: the neighbor kid who still waves at airplanes, the way the dog at the corner bus stop waits for the #38 every morning even though its person died last winter, how moss reclaims a brick at the leisurely speed of patience. Entries accumulate like spare change in a jar that will never buy anything, yet somehow weighs something, anchors the shelf.  

Maybe that’s enough: to keep noticing, to keep testifying, to let the light finish leaving the room and still swear you saw it hesitate.
